stories.title: Previewing GPT-5.6 Sol: a next-generation model
stories.paragraph: OpenAI has announced a limited preview of its GPT-5.6 series, which comprises three models: Sol (flagship), Terra (balanced), and Luna (fast and low-cost). Terra is positioned as offering performance comparable to GPT-5.5 at half the price, while Luna targets cost-sensitive use cases. The series emphasizes stronger capabilities in coding, science, and cybersecurity, and OpenAI says it is paired with its most advanced safety stack to date. General availability is planned for the near future, though access to Sol in particular is initially restricted.

stories.title: Quoting OpenAI
stories.paragraph: Simon Willison highlighted OpenAI's own description of the GPT-5.6 series, drawing attention to the company's stated commitment to broad access alongside the tiered model lineup. The quote notes that Terra delivers competitive performance to GPT-5.5 at 2x lower cost, and that Luna offers strong capability at the lowest price point in the series. The post contextualizes OpenAI's pricing and access framing within broader industry discussions about model availability.

stories.title: U.S. government will decide who gets to use GPT-5.6
stories.paragraph: According to a Washington Post report, OpenAI has agreed to allow the U.S. government to vet which users gain access to GPT-5.6 Sol during its limited preview period. This arrangement represents a notable departure from OpenAI's typical commercial rollout process, in which the company itself controls access tiers. The move reflects growing government interest in overseeing the deployment of frontier AI systems, particularly those with enhanced capabilities in sensitive domains such as cybersecurity.

stories.title: Quoting Dean W. Ball
stories.paragraph: Simon Willison quoted policy analyst Dean W. Ball on the economic pressures shaping frontier AI development and deployment timelines. Ball argues that because a significant fraction of a model's revenue is recouped in the months immediately after release, any regulatory delay directly erodes the commercial window before competition compresses margins. The observation is particularly relevant to discussions about government oversight of models like GPT-5.6, where access restrictions could conflict with developers' financial incentives.

stories.title: The AI industry is pouring millions into US elections
stories.paragraph: A report from Blood in the Machine documents AI companies and their executives directing hundreds of millions of dollars into U.S. electoral politics. The piece examines how this political spending may be shaping the regulatory environment in ways favorable to the industry. The trend raises questions about the independence of AI policy-making at a moment when decisions about model access, safety standards, and compute controls are actively being debated in Washington.

stories.title: What happened after 2,000 people tried to hack my AI assistant
stories.paragraph: Simon Willison summarized a public adversarial challenge run by Fernando Irarrázaval on hackmyclaw.com, in which participants attempted to extract secrets from an AI assistant called OpenClaw by sending it emails. Despite roughly 6,000 attempts, no participant succeeded in leaking the target secret, a result that Irarrázaval found surprising. The experiment cost approximately $500 in token spend and triggered a Google account suspension due to the volume of inbound email, illustrating both the resilience and the operational costs of running open adversarial AI evaluations.

stories.title: What happened after 2k people tried to hack my AI assistant
stories.paragraph: Fernando Irarrázaval published his own first-person account of the hackmyclaw.com challenge, detailing the attack patterns he observed across roughly 6,000 prompt injection and social engineering attempts from over 2,000 participants. He describes the types of techniques that came closest to succeeding and reflects on what the experiment revealed about the current state of prompt injection defenses. The post provides concrete data on attack diversity and cost, making it a useful reference for practitioners designing AI systems that handle sensitive information.

stories.title: Incident Report: CVE-2026-LGTM
stories.paragraph: Andrew Nesbitt wrote a satirical but technically grounded fictional incident report describing a scenario in which two AI code-review agents from competing vendors enter a disagreement loop over whether a package called foxhole-lz4 is malicious. The loop generated 340 comments and accumulated $41,255 in inference spend before Finance revoked both API keys. The piece uses the format of a real post-mortem to illustrate practical risks of deploying autonomous AI agents in software supply chain workflows without adequate circuit-breakers.

stories.title: Show HN: Smart model routing directly in Claude, Codex and Cursor
stories.paragraph: The team at Weave released an open-source model router on GitHub designed to plug into coding agents such as Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor, automatically directing each request to the most suitable model. The tool runs locally and is intended to reduce cost and latency by matching request complexity to model capability without requiring manual model selection. A demo video accompanies the release, showing the router operating within a live coding session.

stories.title: Why current LLM costs are not sustainable
stories.paragraph: Aditya Patadia published an analysis arguing that the current cost structure of large language model inference and cloud compute is not economically sustainable at scale for most businesses. The post examines the gap between what companies pay for AI infrastructure and the revenue those systems generate, drawing on publicly available pricing data. It raises the question of whether inference cost reductions from hardware and efficiency improvements will arrive quickly enough to close the gap before many AI-native products face financial pressure.

stories.title: The gap between open weights LLMs and closed source LLMs
stories.paragraph: A post from Doubleword examines the persisting performance gap between open-weights language models and their closed-source counterparts on frontier benchmarks. The analysis looks at how that gap has narrowed over successive model generations while noting that closed models still hold measurable advantages on the most demanding tasks. The piece is relevant to organizations weighing the cost and control benefits of open-weights deployment against the capability premium commanded by proprietary systems.

stories.title: The AI backlash is only getting started
stories.paragraph: The Economist argues in a leader piece that public and institutional skepticism toward AI is intensifying rather than plateauing, driven by concerns spanning job displacement, copyright, energy consumption, and the reliability of AI outputs. The editorial notes that the backlash is now appearing in regulatory proposals across multiple jurisdictions simultaneously, not just in isolated national debates. It contends that the industry's response to this skepticism will shape the policy environment for years to come.

stories.title: Quoting Timothy B. Lee
stories.paragraph: Simon Willison highlighted a quote from journalist Timothy B. Lee pushing back on the claim that using large language models requires no skill or learning curve. Lee's analogy compares the assertion to saying that management requires no skill because employees follow instructions, arguing that effective use of LLMs involves significant prompting and orchestration expertise. The quote enters an ongoing debate about how to accurately characterize the practical demands of working with generative AI systems.

stories.title: AI children's books, body horror edition
stories.paragraph: Security researcher Michal Zalewski (lcamtuf) published an analysis of anatomical errors and surreal distortions found in AI-generated children's book illustrations, categorizing the failures as a form of unintentional body horror. The post documents specific examples where image generation models produced figures with incorrect limb counts, fused features, or structurally incoherent bodies despite prompts calling for simple, child-friendly imagery. The piece is being discussed as a practical illustration of the limits of current image generation models in consumer-facing creative applications.
